In parallel, generalized additive models themselves have long been used to build inherently interpretable predictors, from classical formulations to recent neural network and tree ensembles. Underlying both lines of work is a classical statistical object: the functional ANOV A decomposition, which, under suitable conditions and even in the presence of dependence, yields a unique additive functional representation of the predictor. Shapley values.Originally introduced in cooperative game theory [ 33], Shapley values [52] were later imported into global sensitivity analysis [ 42, 43] and brought into machine learning [ 56], primarily through SHAP, which subsumes several post-hoc explanation methods such as LIME [50], DeepLIFT [53], and Layer-wise Relevance Propagation [5]. For tree ensembles, TreeSHAP [ 35] computes SHAP values in polynomial time, making them the standard explanation tool for tree-based models [2, 39, 6]. Beyond attribution, several works have connected Shapley values to functional decompositions of machine learning models [8, 24, 25, 38, 6]. However, all such approaches inherit both the estimation difficulties of Shapley values and the criticisms concerning their heuristic design and lack of theoretical justification [31]. Interpretable models.Generalized Additive Models (GAMs) [ 23] are among the most established interpretable model classes, restricting the predictor to a sum of univariate functions of the input features. This additive structure ensures that each component can be visualized and interpreted independently. Several modern variants have been proposed to improve predictive accuracy while preserving interpretability: GA2M [34] extends GAMs with pairwise interactions, later scaled up in the Explainable Boosting Machine (EBM) [41] through cyclic gradient boosting; GAMI-Net [61] learns main effects and pairwise interactions through a structured neural architecture; Neural Additive Models (NAM) [1] and Neural Basis Models (NBM) [ 47] parameterize each component with an individual neural network; and NODE-GAM [11] builds on neural oblivious decision ensembles [46] to combine the flexibility of deep learning with the additive structure of GAMs. Functional ANOV A.The functional ANOV A decomposition was first introduced by [ 26] for independent random variables, resulting in a unique orthogonal decomposition. [ 55] studied its properties under mild regularity conditions and [27] generalized the framework to dependent inputs. [12] and [28] further extended the validity of the decomposition to broader dependence structures, while [48] derived a coupled formulation together with a constructive approximation strategy in this dependent setting. On the estimation side, [32] proposedpure interaction effectsfor tree ensembles but requires knowledge of the input density, while [6] introduced TreeHFD, an efficient algorithm that operates directly from data; however, both approaches are restricted to tree-based models. The constant term ν∅ captures the overall mean effect, the functionsν i represent the marginal effects, and higher-order termsν S with|S| ≥2encode the pure interaction effects among the variables indexed by S. In this paper, we assume that X has a joint densityfwith respect to the Lebesgue measureλ. One can define the components of (1) as the solution of the following optimization problem: {νS}S⊆[p] := argmin {gS ∈L2 S }S⊆[p] Z Rp   X S⊆[p] gS(xS)−ν(x)   2 f(x)dx,(2) subject to thehierarchical orthogonality constraint: ∀T⊊S⊆[p],∀g T ∈L 2 T , Z Rp νS(xS)g T (xT )f(x)dx= 0.(3) Conceptually, decomposition (1) is sought under the constraint that information is non-redundant: for any subset T⊆[p] , the contribution of νS for T⊊S⊆[p] must capture only the variance that cannot be explained by any function of a strict subset of its variables XT . Roughly speaking, each componentν S ispurely an|S|-th order effect, free of any lower-order information. Under mild non-degeneracy conditions on the joint distribution [12, 28], the optimization problem (2) under the constraint (3) admits a unique solution, and the resulting decomposition generally allocates less variance to higher-order components.
